summary: Gruber responds to reader criticism of his earlier mixed review of Avie Tevanian's tenure as Apple's VP of software engineering. He uses the Jaguar Finder's omission of HFS file type and creator code searching as a concrete example of how Tevanian's anti-legacy-Mac policies hurt real users. Despite type/creator searching being useful, fast, easy to implement, and present since System 7.5, Jaguar replaced it with redundant filename extension searching. Gruber argues this was driven by spite and technical politics rather than user benefit, emblematic of Tevanian's pattern of prioritizing NeXT's legacy over Mac usability.
key_points: The Jaguar Finder replaced useful type/creator code searching with redundant 'extension' searching that duplicates existing filename search functionality
File type and creator metadata is stored in HFS directory entries, not resource forks, making searches fast and implementation trivial via existing Carbon APIs
Deprecating a technology means moving away from it going forward, not pretending it never existed — hundreds of billions of files with type/creator codes exist on Macs running Jaguar
The omission exemplifies Tevanian putting technical politics (NeXT vs. classic Mac) ahead of practical usability for real users
Panther 10.3 restored type/creator searching, validating Gruber's criticism but not excusing the Jaguar-era omission
money_quotes: Something useful (type and creator searching) was replaced by something utterly and obviously redundant ("extension" searching).
So (1) type and creator searching is useful; (2) it's been around since System 7.5; (3) it's fast; and (4) it's easy to implement. So why doesn't the Jaguar Finder support it? Spite.
Deprecating only implies moving away from something as you go forward — not pretending that it never existed.
The filename-extension vs. type/creator argument is a political one, and all too often Tevanian has put technical politics ahead of usability.
It's as though it isn't enough that Mac OS X has been deemed a success — but that the old NeXT system must be proven to have been superior all along.
That amounts to hundreds of billions of files with type and creator codes that can't be searched for. How could this limitation possibly be in the interests of regular users?
key_insight: Removing useful legacy features out of ideological hostility rather than genuine technical reasoning is a failure of engineering leadership that puts politics ahead of users.
